speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING FRENCH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Visual History Foundation, February 3, 1995. Survivor, Louis Goldman. Interviewer, James Alexander Bond. New York City, United States of America. And the, the language is English. Thank you. OK, Mr. Goldman, would you please give me your name and spellings as they are now and as they were at your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's going to be a long story. It is now Louis Goldman. That's L-O-U-I-S Goldman. I was born [NON-ENGLISH] Ludwig, which was the traditional giving your sons the names of your two grandfathers. These names then changed as we run from one country to the other. Ludwig in France became Louis. And when we escaped to Italy, it became Luigi. Then in Israel, it became [NON-ENGLISH]. So that I'm now using Louis.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the date of your birth, please.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the location of your birth, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Frankfurt, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's Frankfurt am, am Main. There's another Frankfurt--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --further east called Frankfurt an der Oder.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Could you please give us a little bit about what your life was like before the war in Frankfurt?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't stay long in Frankfurt. I was born there in '25. And we escaped in 1933 as soon as Hitler came to power. And one of my uncles, a brother of my mother, was assassinated by the Brownshirts in Wiesbaden, which is a city not far from Frankfurt. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There's little to tell about my life in, in Frankfurt. I went to school in the Jewish school called the Philanthropin. And we had a nice apartment. That's all I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember of your family? What did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father-- both my parents are from Poland, Lodz, my father actually from the small town north of Lodz called Kutno. But in Lodz he worked for a bank. He was a trader for the bank. And when they came to Germany, he did not work in that capacity. He, he became a traveling salesman for textiles. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So that's what he did in, in, in Frankfurt, too, until we escaped to France.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember of your brother before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] A sweet little kid. He was six years younger than I was. And not, not much except a nice, warm, cozy family life. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There was one child born before I was born, but she died. It was a young little girl. I never knew her. She died in infancy, so that there were only the two of us left, me and my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family very religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but observant. All Jewish holy days were kept. And there was always a nice, warm glow in the family. On Fridays, you know, when Shabbat came and all the holidays were observed, but not in an ultra-Orthodox way-- in a conservative way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe to me what a-- what a regular Sabbath would be like in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [LAUGHS] mother would do the customary gefilte fish. And we lit the candles. And we had the Kiddush. And we had the wine and the challah and the blessings. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] These are the only warm memories I have of a normal life. As young as I was, they're the only two years I remember of what you might call normal family life. But, of course, even at the beginning of 1933, already, I began to, to feel the anti-Semitism, without being much conscious of it. But the fact is that the young German kids would follow us from school and yell insults after us as we were walking home. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] A kind of hatred I didn't understand at the time, but that was the beginning of what was to become a much more vicious atmosphere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember a specific incident of a-- of the violence from the German kids?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Except this following us and, and hurling insults at us, you know. Jews [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] get out of the country. You have no place here. And these were insults spoken by kids who were the same age. So obviously already the massive propaganda indoctrination had already taken-- had already taken root in these young kids. That's the only-- the only manifestation that I could remember of that time. Later on, there were many, many more. But at that, that time, that was the only thing that remains in my memory now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened next-- as soon as my uncle was assassinated, we figured it's time, time to go. And we escaped illegally across the border into France in September, 1933. That's my parents, my brother, and I. And we came to-- we came to Paris, where an organization was already established. And I'm not sure, but I believe it was the Joint, which was commonly referred to as the Joint, but that's a short word for a much longer title. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were given temporary permits of residence, which had to be extended every three or six, six months. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My father, again, without work permit, travelled the suburbs of Paris selling textiles. You didn't have a, a ready-to-wear industry at the time. So people would select the fabric and then go to their tailor to have suits made, or whatever, jackets, pants. So that's what he was selling. And every few months, we would have to go to the prefecture, as it was called, to have the visa extended. And this was at a time when France was flooded with refugees, mainly from Spain, because of the Civil War there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I have experienced nothing but dislike of foreigners from the French authorities at the time. I already spoke French well, but my father didn't. So I went with him every time the visa had to be extended. And it was always a, a, a most unpleasant experience. They made you wait most of the day. And then, when you were finally ushered in front of the functionary who had the supreme power of putting that stamp of extension, he would make it nasty.

speaker: interviewer
text: He would say, you're still here? How much longer are you going to stay? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'll give you another extension. But after that, you have to go, OK? And this was repeated every few, few months.

speaker: interviewee
text: This lasted from 1933 until 1936. In 1936, a new government came to power. The prime minister was Leon Blum. And the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] permits of sojourn were certainly eased. It wasn't such a terrible ordeal to go and have the visa extended and beg for an, an extension. But until 1936, for three long years, it was very, very unpleasant.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your living conditions like in those three years?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had an apartment, rented, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the 13th Arrondissement. And I went to school. There weren't any problems beyond that. Of course, it was strange to be a fellow called Goldman in a classroom full of Dubois and Duponts and Lesueur and-- but I couldn't hide the, the, the fact that I was a foreigner. Plus, of course, there was a subtle distinction to be made there. For all intents and purposes, I came from Germany. And at that time, there was a very strong dislike of the French for the Germans. They had a derogatory term for the Germans called Boches, sal Boches, 'dirty German,' or whatever. I had a hell of a time explaining that I'm not one of them, that I'm actually a refugee from them. I escaped from these-- from these people. But kids of the age of 10, 11, 9 don't make much distinction. My name was German-- Goldman. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was something to be overcome, but it wasn't very serious. It didn't leave any permanent mark on my psyche. But it was there. The need to blend in, to be part of the others, was never granted to me. Although I did make some, some friends. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, well, from 1936 until 1939, it was easier to be a foreigner, a refugee, a Jew, in, in Paris. In 1939, the war started officially-- September 1, 1939, when France and England declared war on Germany after they attacked Poland. But the effects of the war were not felt in, in, in Paris for a long time. They had an expression for it called 'drole de guerre,' which means a funny kind of war, because nothing happened, not on the French front. The Germans were busy in Poland. And then-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm not sure exactly when. I believe April, 1940, they started paying attention to the Western Front. And that's when they started invading Belgium, Holland, Luxembourg. And, of course, the war came closer to France itself. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember any air raids, nothing, until June, 1940. That's when they entered. After having conquered Luxembourg, Holland, and Belgium, they entered the French territory. But we still felt kind of cocky about it, because there was the Maginot Line. I don't know if you ever heard of it. M-A-G-I-N-O-T, which was a superb engineering achievement-- excuse me. It was a fortification line that extended all the way from the German border [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all the way across to Belgium and was supposed to be impregnable. They continually showed pictures of how deep the bunkers were and all these corridors and how everything was geared to repulse any, any attack. The fact is that the Germans took it in something like 24 hours by not even attacking it. They went around it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's when France started to fall apart. And the army, the German armies, came ever closer to Paris. And we decided to escape again. And we left at the last train out of the Gare d'Austerlitz, which was the railroad station, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] joining hundreds and thousands of refugees who just fled because we saw France falling apart all around us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And everybody went south, away from the-- from the Germans. And we wound up in a small town. I believe we were directed there because the train could only go so far. And then when we got out, they said, well, head towards this particular village. So we went there. Very small-- I don't think it had more than a hundred people living there. And we stayed there in a farmhouse that we were given [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] until it was announced that-- though I got to go back for a minute. It's while we were in that small village called Lugagnac that France capitulated. Marechal Petain went on the radio and said, as of tomorrow, the hostilities between France and Germany are terminated. And there will be an armistice signed. This was followed by the division of France into two zones, about half and half. The north was going to be the occupied zone, meaning that German troops were actually stationed on conquered territory, on that part of French conquered territory. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The southern part would be under the authority of the Vichy Government, headed by Petain and his prime minister, Pierre Laval. And that was going to be called the Free Zone.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was announced soon after the capitulation on the armistice that, that was signed that refugees from the north would be allowed to go back. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we left that village in the hope that we would be able to-- because when we left Paris, we abandoned a fully-furnished and equipped three-room apartment. And when we left, we just took a few suitcases, leaving everything behind. So we thought we would get back and retrieve these, these things.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were stopped at the Demarcation Line and-- by the German authorities. And since we did not have French passports-- we were never given in those three years French citizenship-- we were sent, sent back. At that time, there were no yet organized anti-Jewish actions or pogroms or raids, arrests. Just the fact that we did not have French identification papers, we were sent, sent back. Then started, from that time on-- we are now 1940, summer of 1940, fall of 1940-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] going from one place to the other as now [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] anti-Jewish laws were promulgated with certain regularity. First you had to register that you were Jewish. Then you had to-- certain professions you could not exercise, certain places you couldn't live, and so forth. So-- also, arrests started being made in, in, in France of, of Jews. And to be ahead of the gendarmes, we went from one place to the next, various localities all throughout the South of France.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes? You want to ask me something? What can you tell me about the different locations where you were, about the small villages?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. So one was Lugagnac. The other one was Limoges. The third one was Cahors. The fourth one was Florac. The fifth one was Lons-le-Saunier. That's all over the map, this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you stay in these villages?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always rented an apartment or found some kind of abode where we could stay because we never knew how long they would let us stay, you know. The presence of foreigners was immediately obvious. But I still managed in Cahors-- this is one of the places that I mentioned-- to, to, to go to school because I had missed quite a lot of school time, until the day that the gendarmes came and arrested my father and sent him to a concentration camp in the South of France. And the day after, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe it was even on the same afternoon-- that I'm not sure anymore, but definitely within a very short time, either the same day or the day after-- they came and arrested me, my mother, and my brother, and brought us also to that camp in France in, in the South of France. That camp was called Agde, A-G-D-E, right on the Mediterranean. What has always amazed me-- and still does-- is the zeal, I might also say the viciousness, with which the French police and authorities collaborated with the Germans in the apprehension of Jews. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] To give you an example, when we were arrested in Cahors, they thought nothing of delegating two gendarmes to escort us by train-- us meaning my mother, my little brother, and me, barely a teenager at the time-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to escort us all the way, under guard, from Cahors to the camp. And they didn't relax their vigilance until they handed us over to the guards in the camp, which is quite a-- quite a-- quite a trip. Two guards for three people-- a mother and two-- and two children. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was our life in France until we got into the-- into the camp. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The camp was mostly-- it was not an extermination camp. It was a concentration camp, where they assembled Jews for later consigning them over to the-- to the Germans. Also in that camp were thousands of refugees from Spain, from the Civil War.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I managed to get our family out of that camp because I was very upset at having been interrupted from attending school. I was literally yanked out of school on that day when they came to arrest us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get your family out of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: On a certain day, the prefet came to make an inspection, visit. The prefet would be administratively equivalent to the governor. A prefet was the head of what in France is a zone called the departments. France is divided in, I believe, 36 departments. So he was the head of the department. He was the top official. And he came in his gold-braided uniform and with a whole retinue just to see how things are going. And he passed through the camp. And I figured maybe now is my chance. And I waited for a moment, and I blocked his path. And immediately I was surrounded by his guards, and so forth. And I said, I just want to speak to the prefet for a minute, if I may. So the prefet said, let him speak. So I said, please excuse me, but I am very unhappy that I'm kept here, vegetating, when neither I nor my family have done anything wrong. And I was pulled out of school. And while I'm here, wasting my days and weeks, the other kids are learning in, in, in school. And I would very much appreciate a chance to be allowed to continue attending school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was impressed by that, perhaps by the guts I had to stop him. But he said to one of his administrators, he said, give him a pass, him alone, and if he can come back with a guarantee that they will not be a charge to the state and that he has a place-- he and his family, has a place to live, you can let them go. So I was given this pass. And I went to the nearest town which, was Montpellier, M-O-N-T-P-E double L- I-E-R, where I knew there was a French-Jewish organization. See, at that time they were still kind of respectful of French Jews. Their energy had been directed until then at foreign Jews. So at that time, the French Jews were still allowed to exist, sort of. And they had a committee of assistance. So I went there and I explained the situation, and they provided me with a, a place where we could live, which was outside of, of town, but a place, and a guarantee that they would support us for living needs, you know, food and lodging. Equipped with those papers, I went back to the camp. And amazingly, we were left-- we were let go out of the camp under the envious eyes, of course, of everybody else. So we lived in Montpellier, and I went back to school [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] until [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Allies landed in North Africa. At that moment, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Germans invaded the South of France and occupied all of France immediately. What was until now the Free Zone was now occupied, just like the north.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's when we decided it's getting too hot for, for comfort. And we tried to cross the Swiss border. We went to a town near the Swiss border, where we got in touch with some smugglers. And we paid them. And they put us in a pickup truck covered with a tarp. And we went to the border.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at the border, they said, we can't get you across. Why? Because there are German patrols. And they are-- with dogs, they'll pick up the scent. It can't be done. So they had the decency of giving us back the money, but we now went back-- excuse me-- we now went back to the South of France.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at that time, it became known that, in the Italian zone, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Italians did not permit deportation of Jews to, to Germany. Perhaps I should open a parenthesis here. When France capitulated, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Germans, as I said, occupied the north. But the Italians, who were allies with the Germans, occupied a small sliver of land along the Italian border that went from the Riviera, Nice, all the way to the Swiss border.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're running out of tape, no?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It became known that the Italians, although allied with the Germans, would not allow any Jews to be deported from their zone by the Germans. So naturally, what ensued was a mass exodus of Jews from the French-occupied zones gravitating towards the Italian. And if you could make it without being caught by Germans on the way-- passport controls and identifications and traveling permits and all that-- you were, quote, unquote 'safe.' [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My parents and my brother, Harry, managed to get to that zone. I did not try to get there because I had found-- in Montpellier, where I attended school, I had found a couple, man and wife. And they were both photographers. They operated a portrait studio. And they were willing, at great risk to themselves, to hide me there. So I figured I might as well hide somewhere where I can learn something, learn the beginnings of a profession-- excuse me-- rather than being in a safe zone, but, again, doing absolutely nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Should we pause there? Let's pause.
